elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that for a large class of random block lower triangular matrices, the Sylow \break $p$-subgroups of their cokernels have the same constant order fluctuations as that of the matrix products studied by Nguyen and Van Peski~\cite{nguyen2024rank .}
elements.sent-2411-002: We also show that the theorem of Nguyen and Van Peski remains true under a weaker assumption on the number of factors in the matrix products.
elements.sent-2411-003: The cokernel of an $n\times n$ integral matrix $\mathbf{M}$ is defined as \begin{equation}\cok(M)=Z^n/\RowS(M),\end{equation} where $\RowS(\mathbf{M )$ is the subgroup of $\mathbb{Z ^n$ generated by the rows of $\mathbf{M}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: When we study the cokernels of random matrices, we are often interested in the Sylow $p$-subgroup of the cokernel.
elements.sent-2411-005: There is a widespread universality phenomenon for the cokernels of random matrices, that is, in many cases the limiting behavior of the Sylow $p$-subgroup of the cokernel does not depend on the finer details of the given random matrix model.
elements.sent-2411-006: The simplest form of this universality was proved by Wood~\cite{wood2019random : Given $\varepsilon>0$ and a prime $p$, we say that a $\mathbb{Z}$-valued random variable $X$ is $(p,\varepsilon)$-balanced if $\mathbb{P (X\equiv r\mod p)\le 1-\varepsilon$ for all $r\in \mathbb{Z}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Wood proved that if $\mathbf{M _n$ is a random $n\times n$ matrix with independent $(p,\varepsilon)$-balanced entries, then the distribution of the Sylow $p$-group of $\cok(\mathbf{M _n)$ converges to the Cohen-Lenstra distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The Cohen-Lenstra distribution is a distribution on the finite abelian $p$-groups, where the probability assigned to a group $G$ is proportional to $|\Aut(G)|^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-009: This distribution first appeared in conjectures about the distribution of class groups of quadratic number fields~\cite{cohen2006heuristics .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Note that this limiting distribution does not depend on the specific distribution of the entries.
elements.sent-2411-011: Nguyen and Wood~\cite{nguyen2022random weakened the assumptions of the theorem above: It is enough to assume that the entries of $\mathbf{M _n$ are $(p,\varepsilon_n)$-balanced where $\varepsilon_n$ converges to $0$ sufficiently slowly.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The proofs of these theorems rely on the moment method of Wood for random finite abelian groups~\cite{wood2017distribution,wood2022probability .}
elements.sent-2411-013: If a random matrix model has some additional algebraic structure, then this algebraic structure is also reflected in the distribution of the cokernel.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, for symmetric matrices, the cokernel can be equipped with a perfect, symmetric, bilinear pairing.
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, to obtain the limiting distribution for the cokernels of symmetric matrices, one needs to modify the Cohen-Lenstra distribution by taking into account the number of such pairings~\cite{clancy2015note,clancy2015cohen,wood2017distribution .}
elements.sent-2411-016: A similar phenomenon occurs for skew symmetric matrices~\cite{delaunay2001heuristics,bhargava2015modeling,nguyen2022local , and for $p$-adic Hermitian matrices~\cite{lee2023universality .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: We again see that the limiting behavior of the cokernel is universal: it depends on the symmetry class of the random matrix model, but not on the specific distribution of the entries.
elements.sent-2411-018: See \cite{recent2,recent3,recent4,recent5,recent6,recent7,recent8,meszaros2020distribution,meszaros2023cohen,meszaros2024Zpband,gorokhovsky2024time,kang2024random for further results on the cokernels of random matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Nguyen and Van Peski~\cite{nguyen2024universality,nguyen2024rank considered matrix products of the form $\mathbf{A _1\mathbf{A _2\cdots \mathbf{A _k$, where $\mathbf{A _1,\dots,\mathbf{A _k$ are i.i.d.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: For fixed~$k$, Nguyen and Van Peski showed that as $n\to\infty$, $\Gamma$ has a limiting distribution~\cite{nguyen2024universality .}
elements.sent-2411-022: This limiting distribution is a modified version of the Cohen-Lenstra distribution where the probability assigned to a group $G$ is obtained by also taking into account the number of chains of subgroups of~$G$ of length~$k$.
elements.sent-2411-023: When $k$ goes to infinity together with $n$, the size of $\Gamma$ goes to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-024: Thus, $\Gamma$ can not have a limiting distribution in the usual sense.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, Nguyen and Van Peski~\cite{nguyen2024rank proved that if $n,k\to \infty$ such that $k=O(e^{(\log n)^{1-\varepsilon )$, then the fluctuation of $\Gamma$ stays of constant order.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: They also determined the limiting distribution of these fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-027: The limit again does not depend on the distribution of the entries.
elements.sent-2411-028: See Theorem~\ref{nguyenvanPeski for a more detailed statement.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This is a new interesting phenomenon, so it is a natural question whether we can see the same type of constant order fluctuations for other random groups of growing order.
elements.sent-2411-030: Let us consider the block lower bidiagonal matrix \begin{equation}C= A_1 0 \ddots I A_2 0 \ddots 0 I A_3 0 \ddots \ddots 0 I A_4 0 \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots 0 I A_k .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}C= A_1 0 \ddots I A_2 0 \ddots 0 I A_3 0 \ddots \ddots 0 I A_4 0 \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots \ddots 0 I A_k .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-031: One can prove that \begin{equation}\label{cokidentity} \cok(\mathbf{C})\cong \cok(\mathbf{A}_1\mathbf{A}_2\cdots \mathbf{A}_k),\end{equation} see Section~\ref{appendix .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Thus, $\cok(\mathbf{C )$ shows the same behavior as the cokernel of matrix products.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Given the prevalence of the universality phenomenon in the study of cokernels of random matrices, one could expect the same behavior from many other random block lower triangular matrix models.
elements.sent-2411-034: Theorem~\ref{thmmain below provides such examples of block lower triangular matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, the hidden block lower triangular structure of matrix products gives a more general context where the constant order fluctuations found by Nguyen and Van Peski can be observed universally.
elements.sent-2411-036: Now we describe our setting: Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a subset of primes, and let $\varepsilon>0$.
elements.sent-2411-037: We say that a $\mathbb{Z}$-valued random variable $X$ is $(\mathcal{P ,\varepsilon)$-balanced if \begin{equation}P(X\equiv r\mod p)\le 1-\varepsilon\qquad for all p\in P and  r\inZ.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}P(X\equiv r\mod p)\le 1-\varepsilon\qquad for all p\in P and  r\inZ.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-038: We say that a random matrix over $\mathbb{Z}$ is $(\mathcal{P ,\varepsilon)$-balanced if it has independent $(\mathcal{P ,\varepsilon)$-balanced entries.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Let $\mathbf{A ^{(h) =(\mathbf{A ^{(h) _{i,j )_{1\le i,j\le k(h)}$ and $\mathbf{B ^{(h) =(\mathbf{B ^{(h) _{i,j )_{1\le i,j\le k(h)}$ be sequences of random block matrices over the integers such that the blocks $\mathbf{A ^{(h) _{i,j}$ and $\mathbf{B ^{(h) _{i,j}$ are $n_i(h)\times n_j(h)$ matrices.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-045: Given a finite abelian group $G$ and a nonnegative integer $i$, let $c(G,i)$ be the number of chains of subgroups $\{0\}\subsetneq H_1\subsetneq H_2\subsetneq \cdots\subsetneq H_i\subseteq G$ of length $i$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The largest possible length $\ell(G)$ of such a chain can be obtained as $\ell(G)=\sum e_i$, where $|G|=\prod p_i^{e_i}$ is the prime factorization of~$|G|$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $G$ be a finite abelian group, let $\mathcal{P}$ be the set of prime divisors of $|G|$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Then for any sequence of random matrices $\mathbf{C ^{(h)}$ satisfying the assumptions \ref{A1 --\ref{A7 above, we have \begin{equation}\lim_{h\to\infty} \frac{E|\Hom(\cok(C^{(h)}),G)|}{k(h)^{\ell(G)}}=\frac{c(G,\ell(G))}{\ell(G)!}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The expectations $\mathbb{E |\Hom(\cok(\mathbf{C ^{(h) ),G)|$ are usually called the Hom-moments of $\cok(\mathbf{C ^{(h) )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Note that the limiting rescaled moments do not depend on the finer details of our random matrix models such as the sizes of the blocks or the exact distribution of the entries.
elements.sent-2411-051: This is another manifestation of the universality phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-052: Combining Theorem~\ref{homlemma with a special case of the rescaled moment method of Nguyen and Van Peski~\cite{nguyen2024rank stated in Theorem~\ref{rescaledmomentmethod , one can describe the limiting fluctuations of the Sylow $p$-subgroup of $\cok(\mathbf{C ^{(h) )$, as we explain next.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Any partition $\lambda=(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\dots,\lambda_r)$ gives rise to a finite abelian $p$-group \begin{equation}G_{\lambda}=\bigoplus_{i=1}^r Z/p^{\lambda_i}Z.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: This gives a one-to-one correspondence between partitions and finite abelian $p$-groups.
elements.sent-2411-056: Note that if $\lambda'$ is the conjugate of the partition $\lambda$, that is, $\lambda'_i=|\{j:\lambda_j\ge i\}|$, then $\lambda_i'$ also has a group theoretic meaning, namely, \begin{equation}\label{conjugate}\lambda_i'=\text{rank}(p^{i-1} G_\lambda).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: Thus, the Sylow $p$-subgroup of the cokernel of an integral random matrix, which is a finite abelian $p$-group can be also represented by a partition, or equivalently by the corresponding Young diagram.
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, a random integral matrix determines a random Young diagram.
elements.sent-2411-061: Theorem~\ref{thmmain below describes the joint fluctuations of the first $d$ columns of the Young diagram corresponding the Sylow $p$-group of $\cok(\mathbf{C ^{(h) )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Before we can state this theorem, we need a few further notions.
elements.sent-2411-063: Given a positive integer $d$, let \begin{equation}\Sig_d =\{(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\dots,\lambda_d)\in Z^d\,:\, \lambda_1\ge\lambda_2\cdots\ge \lambda_d\} and  \Sig_d^{\ge 0} =\{(\lambda_1,\lambda_2,\dots,\lambda_d)\in Z^d\,:\, \lambda_1\ge\lambda_2\cdots\ge \lambda_d\ge 0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: Note that $\Sig_d^{\ge 0}$ is just the set of partitions with at most $d$ parts.
elements.sent-2411-065: For $\chi>0$, let $\mathcal{L _{d,p^{-1 ,\chi}$ be a $\Sig_d\,$-valued random variable such that \begin{equation}\mathlarger{E p^{\left\langle L_{d,p^{-1},\chi}\,,\,\lambda \right\rangle}}=\frac{((p-1)\chi)^{|\lambda|}}{|\lambda|!}c(G_{\lambda'},|\lambda|)\qquad  for all \lambda\in\Sig_d^{\ge 0}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-083: The following lemma is well known, see for example \cite[Proposition 5.2.]{meszaros2020distribution .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Let $\mathbf{M}$ be an $n\times n$ random matrix over $\mathbb{Z}$, and let $G$ be a finite abelian group.. Then \begin{equation}E|\Hom(\cok(M),G)|=\sum_{g\in G^n} P(Mg=0).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-085: For a vector $\mathbf{g}\in G^n$, let $\langle\mathbf{g \rangle$ be the subgroup of $G$ generated by the components of $\mathbf{g}$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: There is a $c>0$ such that for all large enough $n$, if $\mathbf{M}$ is a $(\mathcal{P ,\varepsilon)$-balanced $n\times n$ random matrix and $G_0$ is a subgroup of $G$, then \begin{equation}\exp(-\exp(-cn))\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G_0^n\\\langleg\rangle=G_0}} \min_{f\in G_0^n} P(Mg=f)\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G_0^n\\\langleg\rangle=G_0}} \max_{f\in G_0^n} P(Mg=f)\le \exp(\exp(-cn)).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\exp(-\exp(-cn))\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G_0^n\\\langleg\rangle=G_0}} \min_{f\in G_0^n} P(Mg=f)\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G_0^n\\\langleg\rangle=G_0}} \max_{f\in G_0^n} P(Mg=f)\le \exp(\exp(-cn)).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-087: Wood~\cite[Theorem 2.9]{wood2019random proved that there are $c_0,K$ depending only on $G$ and $\varepsilon$ such that for any $(\mathcal{P ,\varepsilon)$-balanced $n\times n$ random matrix $\mathbf{M}$, we have \begin{equation}1-K\exp(-c_0 n)\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G^n\\\langleg\rangle=G}} P(Mg=0)\le 1+K\exp(-c_0 n).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: One can see that the same proof actually gives the stronger statement that \begin{equation}\label{generalWood}1-K\exp(-c_0 n)\le \sum_{\substack{\mathbf{g}\in G^n\\\langle\mathbf{g}\rangle=G}} \min_{\mathbf{f}\in G^n} \mathbb{P}(\mathbf{M}\mathbf{g}=\mathbf{f})\le \sum_{\substack{\mathbf{g}\in G^n\\\langle\mathbf{g}\rangle=G}} \max_{\mathbf{f}\in G^n} \mathbb{P}(\mathbf{M}\mathbf{g}=\mathbf{f})\le 1+K\exp(-c_0 n).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\label{generalWood}1-K\exp(-c_0 n)\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G^n\\\langleg\rangle=G}} \min_{f\in G^n} P(Mg=f)\le \sum_{\substack{g\in G^n\\\langleg\rangle=G}} \max_{f\in G^n} P(Mg=f)\le 1+K\exp(-c_0 n).\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-157: We say that $\mathbf{g}\in G^n$ is a code of distance $w$, if for all $X\subset \{1,2,\dots,n\}$ such that $|X|<w$, we have \begin{equation}\langle g(i)\,:\,i\in \{1,2,\dots,n\}\setminus X\rangle = G.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-162: This is consistent with our definition of $\ell(G)$ in the Introduction in the sense that $\ell(G)=\ell(|G|)$.
elements.sent-2411-163: Given $\delta>0$ and $\mathbf{g}\in G^n$, the $\delta$-depth of $\mathbf{g}$ is the maximal positive $D$ such that there is an $X\subset\{1,2,\dots,n\}$ satisfying $|X|<\ell(D)\delta n$ and \begin{equation}\left[G\,:\,\langle g(i)\,:\,i\in \{1,2,\dots,n\}\setminus X\rangle\right]=D.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-164: If there is not any such $D$, the $\delta$-depth of $\mathbf{g}$ is defined to be $1$.
elements.sent-2411-165: There is a constant $K$ depending $G$ such that for all $D>1$, we have at most \begin{equation}K{{n}\choose{\lceil\ell(D)\delta n-1\rceil}} |G|^n D^{-n+\ell(D)\delta n}\end{equation} vectors in $G^n$ with $\delta$-depth $D$.

elements.sent-2411-207: Combining this with \eqref{Hsize and \ref{A5 , we obtain \begin{equation}\label{w0ti} \sum_{\substack{\mathbf{g}\in G^n w(\mathbf{g})=0 t(\mathbf{g})=i}}\mathbb{P}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{g}=0)=(1+o_h(1))c(G,i){{k}\choose{i}}=(1+o_h(1))c(G,i)\frac{k^i}{i!}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: Thus, \begin{equation}\label{limitmoment9}\lim_{h\to\infty}(k(h))^{-\ell(G)}\sum_{\substack{\mathbf{g}\in G^{n(h)} w(\mathbf{g})=0 t(\mathbf{g})=i}}\mathbb{P}(\mathbf{C}^{(h)}\mathbf{g}=0)=\begin{cases}\frac{c(G,\ell(G))}{\ell(G)!} \text{if }i=\ell(G), 0 \text{otherwise}.\end{cases}\end{equation} Using Lemma~\ref{lemmacokmoment , we see that \begin{equation}\frac{E|\Hom(\cok(C),G)|}{k^{\ell(G)}} =k^{-\ell(G)}\sum_{g\in G^n} P(Cg=0) =k^{-\ell(G)}\left(\sum_{\substack{g\in G^n w(g)\ge 1}} P(Cg=0)+\sum_{i=0}^{\ell(G)}\sum_{\substack{g\in G^{n} w(g)=0 t(g)=i}}P(Cg=0)\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-087: \begin{equation}\frac{E|\Hom(\cok(C),G)|}{k^{\ell(G)}} =k^{-\ell(G)}\sum_{g\in G^n} P(Cg=0) =k^{-\ell(G)}\left(\sum_{\substack{g\in G^n w(g)\ge 1}} P(Cg=0)+\sum_{i=0}^{\ell(G)}\sum_{\substack{g\in G^{n} w(g)=0 t(g)=i}}P(Cg=0)\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-209: Then following along the lines of the proof of Theorem~\ref{homlemma , it can be proved that \begin{equation}\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{E|\Hom(\Gamma_n,G)|}{k^{\ell(G)}}=\frac{c(G,\ell(G))}{\ell(G)!}.\end{equation}.}
